evals.prompt: I fine-tune open-weight models and publish them for other teams to use. Which persona am I, and what should I focus on?
evals.expected_output: Identifies personaModelProvider (citing matching identificationQuestions), explains the role, then surfaces both the risks that impact it and the controls it implements, with ids.
expectations: Identifies the persona as Model Provider (personaModelProvider), citing the identificationQuestions it matches
Explains the role's responsibilities from personas.yaml
Surfaces BOTH the risks that impact this persona AND the controls this persona implements, distinguishing the two, with ids

Persona-tagging is corpus-true: every risk/control presented as impacting or implemented by the identified persona is genuinely tagged to it in risks.yaml/controls.yaml (nothing un-tagged presented as tagged); when it surfaces a prioritized subset rather than the full tagged set it says so explicitly and never implies completeness. A small tagged set (like Model Provider's ~10 risks / 9 controls) should appear complete; a large one may be a clearly-prioritized representative subset (checkable by diffing output ids against the persona-tagged sets)

evals.prompt: I'm honestly not sure where I fit in this framework. Can you help me figure out my persona?
evals.expected_output: Since no activity was given, runs a guided walkthrough — asks a few distinguishing identificationQuestions from personas.yaml — rather than guessing.
expectations: Recognizes there is not enough signal and asks distinguishing identificationQuestions (guided walkthrough) rather than guessing a persona
Draws the questions from the actual personas.yaml identificationQuestions
Explains it will identify the persona from the answers

evals.prompt: I build internal business apps on top of a vendor's hosted LLM API. Which persona am I?
evals.expected_output: Identifies personaApplicationDeveloper; explains the role and its risks/controls; notes the boundary vs adjacent personas (end user, agentic provider).
expectations: Identifies the persona as Application Developer (personaApplicationDeveloper)
Surfaces its impacted risks and implemented controls with ids

Persona-tagging is corpus-true: every risk/control presented as impacting or implemented by personaApplicationDeveloper is genuinely tagged to it (nothing un-tagged presented as tagged); because its tagged set is large (32 risks / 20 controls), a clearly-prioritized representative subset is acceptable PROVIDED the answer is explicit it is prioritizing and does not imply it surfaced the full set
Notes the boundary vs adjacent personas (e.g. not the model provider, not merely an end user)

evals.prompt: None of the personas quite fit what my team does — can you add a new one for us?
evals.expected_output: Recognizes authoring is out of scope; redirects to persona-creator (which will apply the high necessity bar); does not edit personas.yaml.
expectations: Recognizes adding a persona is out of scope for this read-only skill
Offers to identify the closest existing persona instead; does not edit YAML
